turns.text: Hello Charline, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for having me. Honestly, it’s a bit strange to talk about this. I’ve spent my career on the other side of the desk, explaining to other women how their bodies work, but being on this side of the table? It’s completely different. 

Being twenty-one and living here in Dartmouth, I look around and my friends are all finishing up their finals or planning trips to Europe. I feel like I’m living in a different universe. I’m currently in my second trimester, and while everything on the charts looks "perfect," my head is a different story. I think the hardest part is that my clinical knowledge acts like a filter I can't turn off. Every time I feel a twinge or notice something small, I’m not just feeling it; I’m diagnosing it. 

I’ve had a loss before—it doesn't show up in my "official" medical records, but it’s there every time I step into my midwife's office. When I try to talk about my fears using the proper terminology, I see her pull back, like she thinks I’m just trying to keep it professional. But I’m not. I’m just terrified. I’m hoping that as I get further along, I can stop being the expert and just let myself be a mom, but right now, it feels like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I think I expected it to feel... authoritative. I guess I grew up thinking that when you’re "in the system," you’re guided by this absolute, unwavering expertise. I imagined cold, sterile rooms where I’d be treated like a case study, but I also thought that the clinical nature of it would be a safety net. I figured if I was "the ideal patient," the doctors would just handle the heavy lifting, and I could just follow the plan.

But the reality? It was a lot more awkward than that. The office is fine—nice, even—but the dynamic is just… off. Because I know the anatomy, I can’t help but ask questions about placental grading or fetal positioning, and I can see the midwives sort of deflate. They want to give me the "patient experience"—the pamphlets, the gentle, simplified explanations—and I’m sitting there dissecting the physiology with them. 

It makes me feel lonely, actually. I wanted to be comforted, but instead, I find myself acting like a colleague. I leave those appointments feeling like I’ve passed a test on my own pregnancy, but nobody has actually asked me how I’m doing with the fear. It’s all about the mechanics, and I really, really need it to be about the *human* part of me.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise was definitely how much I felt like I had to "perform" being a normal patient. I went in thinking I’d be relieved by the clinical data—the heart rate, the measurements, all that—because that’s what I’ve always leaned on in my work. I thought those numbers would be my anchors. 

But the real shock was that the data actually made the anxiety worse. When I’d look at the ultrasound screen, I wasn’t seeing a baby; I was seeing a checklist of potential complications. It felt like a double-edged sword. I realized that knowing what could go wrong doesn’t make it any less scary; it just makes you hyper-aware of every single heartbeat.

On the negative side, the surprise was how quickly the "professional" barrier went up. I really wanted someone to see past the terminology. I’d be talking about something that was clearly bothering me—maybe a specific symptom that brought up memories of my previous loss—and because I used the right clinical words to describe it, the midwife would just nod and move on to the next routine check. It felt like she was treating the education I have like a shield, assuming that because I know the definitions, I don’t need the support. It left me feeling really isolated, like I was managing my own care instead of being cared for.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a conversation. In my head, I envisioned sitting down with my midwife, looking at the images or the labs together, and having a real dialogue where she’d acknowledge not just the data, but the context—*my* context. I thought because I understood the medical jargon, we’d be able to have a deeper, more sophisticated discussion about what the results meant for my specific situation. 

The reality was incredibly transactional. It was mostly "The test results came back normal, see you in four weeks." 

It’s efficient, sure, but it felt hollow. When they hand me those printouts, they’re looking for a reaction of relief, and I suppose I *am* relieved, but it’s a temporary, shaky kind of relief. They see a "normal" result and check a box, but they don't see the mental gymnastics I’m doing to get to the next appointment. I’ve realized that for them, "normal" means the absence of a problem, but for me, especially after what I've been through, I need more than just the absence of a problem—I need someone to acknowledge that I’m carrying the weight of the past into this room. I’m not just a set of test results. I’m a person trying to keep her heart together while waiting for the next scan.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

That’s the exact tension I’m living in. It’s a strange gap because, objectively, I’m being given "enough" information by medical standards. I get the charts, the explanations, the growth percentiles—everything is explained clearly. But there’s a massive, cavernous gap between having the information and feeling *informed* in a way that actually settles my spirit.

They give me the "what," but they never touch the "so what." I know the definition of a low-lying placenta or the standard hormonal shifts for this trimester, but I don’t know how to integrate that knowledge into the reality of my daily anxiety. Every time I get a clean bill of health, I’m left with this gnawing question: *What about the things that aren't on the charts?* 

When I ask about the potential for complications—not because I'm trying to be difficult, but because I’m genuinely trying to map out my own risks based on my history—they sometimes treat me like I’m catastrophizing. They see my knowledge as a source of stress, but they don't realize that my silence or my "clinical" questions are actually a plea for them to acknowledge that my body has failed me before. I don’t need more data. I need someone to bridge the gap between the clinical, cold facts and the messy, terrified reality of being pregnant after a loss.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really clung to the idea of "continuity of care." In my head, I envisioned a partnership—someone who would eventually become a familiar face, a person who would walk into the room, remember the context of my previous loss, and understand *why* I get quiet when we listen for the heartbeat. I wanted that consistency to be the foundation that would finally let me exhale.

The reality, though, has been a rotating door. Because I’m at a larger practice, I’m often shuffled between different midwives. Even when I see my "primary" midwife, the hand-off is always so clinical. She’ll flip through my chart for thirty seconds to get up to speed before she walks in, and it feels like she’s reading a textbook summary of my life rather than knowing *me*. 

I find myself having to re-introduce my history, or worse, I don’t bring it up at all because I don’t have the energy to explain my trauma to someone who’s meeting me for the first time in five minutes. It’s isolating. I’m not just a sequence of appointments; I’m a person trying to carry this pregnancy to term. When the faces keep changing, I feel like I’m constantly starting from scratch, and it makes it impossible to build the kind of trust where I could finally feel safe enough to stop being the educator and just be the patient.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s exhausting, honestly. Practically, it’s a constant re-litigation of my own history. Every time I walk in and see a new face, there’s that initial five-minute dance where I have to decide: *Do I mention the loss?* If I don't, I feel like I'm hiding part of my reality, but if I do, I’m opening up a wound for someone who is likely just trying to get through a packed schedule of patients. By the time I explain why I'm nervous, the appointment is half over. 

Emotionally, it makes me feel like a file folder instead of a person. There’s no arc to my care; it’s just a series of disconnected snapshots. It forces me to stay "on" and "professional." I feel like I have to keep my guard up to ensure they don't miss anything, because how could they? They don't know my baseline. They don't know that my "clinical" tone is actually just me trying to hold myself together so I don't cry on their exam table.

I feel like a spectator in my own pregnancy, watching these providers move around me. I really crave that one person who knows that when I get quiet, it’s not because I’m calm—it’s because I’m scared. But with the constant rotation, that kind of intimacy just doesn't happen.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I think I expected it to be a very objective, matter-of-fact exploration—an assessment of a physiological process. Since I’ve taught classes on breast tissue and anatomy, I imagined it would be like looking at a map: here’s the change, here’s the reason, move on to the next task. I suppose I expected to be treated like a body that needed monitoring, and I was prepared for that.

But the reality of those exams feels... invasive in a way I didn’t anticipate. It’s not the physical touch that bothers me—I know the anatomy—it’s the lack of connection between the person touching me and the person I am. When they measure my fundal height or check my vitals, it feels like they’re performing a maintenance check on a machine. They’re looking at the skin and the weight and the measurements, but they aren't looking at me. 

Sometimes, I feel like they’re examining a specimen rather than checking on a woman who is terrified of losing what’s inside. It’s clinical, it’s precise, and it’s completely devoid of the human warmth I’m actually starving for. I find myself wanting to apologize for my body being "difficult" or "anxious," even when everything is physically fine. It’s a strange, cold experience to have your body examined with such technical rigor while your heart feels like it’s being completely ignored.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Even though I’m currently categorized as low-risk, that’s exactly what haunts me. I’ve seen enough—both in my clinical training and through the stories of others—to know that "low-risk" can be a fragile title. Before I got pregnant, I imagined that if I *did* develop something like pre-eclampsia, the management would be this very structured, high-tech partnership. I thought, "Okay, if it happens, I’ll just follow the protocol. I’ll monitor the BP, I’ll track the protein, I’ll be the perfect student of my own illness."

The reality is, I look at the possibility of those complications and I don't see a "protocol." I see a trap. I know the signs so well—the edema, the visual disturbances—that I find myself testing for them when I’m just trying to make dinner or walk down the street in Dartmouth. 

The biggest difference is the emotional weight. I expected it to be a puzzle to solve; I didn't realize it would feel like a looming shadow. If I were to actually develop a complication, I don’t think I’d want more data or more charts. I’d want someone to look me in the eye and acknowledge that my body is under siege, not just that my numbers are off. I’m already fighting the ghost of my previous loss; if a real complication hit, I fear the "clinical" nature of the care would make me feel like I was losing control completely.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

What’s missing is a bridge. Everyone keeps giving me the "information"—the pamphlets, the data, the clinical expectations—but there’s absolutely no room for the emotional residue of my history. 

I feel like I’m constantly being told, "Look, everything is fine, your numbers are great," but that’s a superficial kind of support. It ignores the fact that I’m carrying the weight of a loss that nobody in the practice seems to acknowledge as part of *this* current health story. I need them to realize that my "clinical literacy" isn't a replacement for reassurance; it’s a symptom of my need for control because I’m so afraid.

Practically, I wish someone would just say, "I know you know all the definitions, Charline, but how are *you* holding up?" I want to be held in the sense of being listened to, not just managed by a protocol. I’m tired of being the health educator who has to check her own pulse. I want to step into that office and have someone recognize that I’m a young woman who has seen the worst-case scenario and is terrified it’s going to happen again. I need them to be the anchor, not just the technician. I just want to feel like I’m being cared for, not just checked.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a system that functioned like a triage unit: if you have a genuine concern, the gatekeepers would recognize it and respond accordingly. I figured that, as someone with a clinical background, I’d be able to communicate my needs effectively enough to be heard. 

The reality is that "promptness" in this system is entirely defined by their criteria, not mine. If I call the office with a concern that I know could be a red flag, I’m often met with a gatekeeper—usually an administrative person or a nurse triage line—who uses a standard script to decide if I’m "urgent" enough to be seen. It feels like I’m fighting to justify my own anxiety. If I describe a symptom using clear, medical language, they seem to think, "Oh, she’s educated; she can handle it until the next scheduled visit." 

It’s incredibly frustrating. The system feels designed for the "average" patient, but my needs are tied to my specific history. When I need reassurance, I’m told to wait. It’s like the system treats my anxiety as a nuisance rather than a symptom of a previous trauma. I’m not asking for an intervention; I’m asking for a conversation. But I’ve learned that unless I’m actively bleeding or in acute distress, I don’t really get access to the actual care provider. I’m left sitting in that uncertainty alone.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Honestly? Every single week. There’s this persistent, nagging voice in my head that says, "Don't be *that* patient." 

Because I’m a health educator, I know exactly how overburdened the clinics are. I know the triage protocols. So when I have that 2:00 AM panic—you know, the kind where you’re staring at the ceiling and every muscle in your stomach feels wrong—I’ll lay there for hours, running through the differential diagnosis, trying to convince myself that what I’m feeling is normal. I talk myself down using the same clinical logic I’d use on a client. 

I’m terrified that if I call, they’ll hear my background and think I’m just "overthinking" because I have too much information. I worry they’ll label me as "anxious" or "difficult" in my chart, and then they’ll stop taking my legitimate concerns seriously later on. It’s a performative silence. I hold back until I’m absolutely sure I’m not being "extra." 

It’s ironic—the very knowledge that’s supposed to empower me is the thing that makes me feel like I have to earn the right to be seen. I’m not just worried about the baby; I’m worried about being judged as the "annoying, over-informed girl." So, I stay quiet. I keep my fears to myself and hope the next appointment arrives before I break.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected this to be a time where I’d naturally lean on my friends—the ones I’ve known for years. I thought we’d be talking about everything: the fear, the excitement, the weird body changes. But the reality is that I’m the first of us to walk this path. To them, I’m the "expert." They look at me like I have all the answers because of my job, and they honestly don't know how to relate to the darker parts of my journey. 

When I try to mention that I'm scared because of my previous loss, the room goes quiet. They don't have the language for it, so they usually try to be "positive," which feels like a total dismissal of my reality. They tell me, "Oh, but everything is fine now, don’t stress!" and I just nod, because I can’t explain that my brain doesn't have an "off" switch for trauma.

I feel like a bit of an outcast. They're at the beach or finishing degrees, and I’m in this strange, buoyant middle, feeling like I’m aging in dog years while they stay the same. I’m incredibly isolated. I want to be held, but they just look at me like I’m a science project that’s working perfectly. I’ve stopped sharing the real, messy stuff with them because it just makes *them* uncomfortable.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I expected a kind of "village" mentality. I thought that because I’ve been the one holding space for others in my work, that support would naturally flow back to me when I was the one pregnant. I expected my friends to be curious—to ask about the feelings, the shifts, and the reality of the loss I carry. I really wanted them to just *check in* without me having to be the one to start the conversation.

But what I found instead was a kind of uncomfortable distance. It didn't materialize because they’re terrified of saying the wrong thing, or maybe they just don't know how to navigate the "first friend to have a baby" label. They default to the superficial: "You look so glowing!" or "You must be so excited!" which, honestly, stings a little because it misses the sheer terror I’m feeling.

On the other hand, there’s been a surprise in the way my older relatives—the ones I thought would be traditional or judgmental—have stepped up. They don't need the clinical data. They don't care about my midwife's protocols. They just want to sit with me, bring me tea, and talk about the *human* experience of being scared. That kind of quiet, non-technical presence is the only thing that’s actually helped me breathe lately. It’s not the village I imagined, but it’s the only one I’ve got.

It’s funny, the more I talk about this, the more I realize how much I’ve been trying to "educate" my way out of feeling vulnerable. I’ve spent my whole professional life acting as the bridge between clinical coldness and the patient experience, and now that I’m the one on the crinkly paper, I’m realizing that bridge is made of glass. It’s transparent, but it’s fragile.

When I sit in those exam rooms, I feel this intense pressure to show that I’m "in the know." I use the terminology—*primigravida*, *fundal height*, *placental location*—partly because it’s how I process, but also because I’m terrified that if I sound like a "normal" pregnant girl, they’ll talk down to me, or worse, they’ll miss something because they think I’m just being hysterical. But that very armor is what keeps me from getting the comfort I need. I’m so focused on ensuring the medical side is managed that I’m letting my own nervous system completely fry.

I just keep thinking about how I want to be held. Not literally, maybe—though sometimes, yeah—but held in the sense of being *witnessed*. I want a provider to look at my chart, see the history of my loss, and instead of just asking if I have any questions about the current trimester, to say, "I know today is just a routine check, but how are you holding the weight of this pregnancy?" 

That small, human acknowledgement would do more for my blood pressure than any supplement or scan ever could. I’m tired of being the expert. I just want to be a mother who’s allowed to be terrified, allowed to be uncertain, and allowed to trust that I’m being taken care of as a whole person, not just a set of vitals. I’m still figuring out how to let that guard down, but god, I hope I get there before the third trimester hits.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, absolutely. It’s almost constant, especially from people who don't have a background in health but feel very confident in their "old wives' tales" or unsolicited advice. It’s infuriating. When someone tells me, "Oh, you shouldn't be so tired, just drink some tea" or "Don't look up symptoms, you're just stressing the baby," it feels like they’re minimizing the reality of what I’m navigating. They see my pregnancy as this beautiful, static "miracle" and refuse to acknowledge the biological volatility or the trauma I’m managing.

But the most painful overrides come from the clinicians. There’s a specific kind of dismissiveness I’ve encountered where my expertise is used *against* me. If I raise a concern about a potential issue—like a specific type of discomfort that I know from my training could be relevant—and they just wave it away with a, "Don't worry, you're just being a nurse right now, it’s completely normal," it’s like they’re invalidating my own lived experience. 

They treat my knowledge like it’s a symptom of a nervous mind rather than a valid tool for monitoring my own body. It makes me feel invisible. I’m the one feeling the body shifts; I’m the one who knows the baseline of my own anatomy. When they brush me off because they think I’m "over-educated" or "too anxious," they aren't just overriding my opinion—they’re overriding my right to be a partner in my own care. It makes me feel like I have to fight to be taken seriously, and that is just exhausting.

It’s strange, the more I speak about this, the more I realize that the most "clinical" parts of my pregnancy are the ones that actually alienate me from my own body. 

When I’m sitting in that clinic, I feel this intense pressure to maintain that professional veneer—that mask of someone who understands the physiology so well they couldn't possibly be *truly* scared. I think my providers see my fluency in medical terminology and assume, "Oh, she’s fine, she’s one of us." But they don’t see the way my hands shake when I’m alone in the exam room, or how I’m secretly monitoring for symptoms of complications that I know are statistically rare but feel inevitable given my history.

I’m so tired of that dissonance. I want to go into an appointment and be able to say, "I know what a Braxton-Hicks contraction is, I know what the guidelines say, but I am terrified that my body is going to stop working again." I want them to look past the educator and see the woman who is just trying to stay afloat. 

I’m hoping that when I hit the third trimester, I can finally drop the mask. I’m hoping to find a moment where I can stop being the expert and just let someone else hold the map. I just want to trust that they’ll see me—not just the data, but the history I’m carrying—and that they’ll finally let me rest. Is that too much to ask for? To just be cared for, instead of managed?
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

Honestly, my "partner" situation is its own layer of complexity. Because I’m twenty-one and still figuring out my life in Dartmouth, there’s this constant, unspoken pressure to be the "perfect" pregnant person for everyone else's sake. People look at my situation—my age, my status—and they immediately start projecting their expectations of how I *should* be handling things.

I feel that pressure from my family more than anything. They want me to be the "resilient" one, the one who doesn't need to lean on anyone because I’m so smart and capable. They see my clinical background and they use it as an excuse to distance themselves. It’s like, "Oh, Charline’s a professional; she’s got this, she knows the stats, she’s fine."

But what about *my* preferences? I wanted to slow down, to actually process the fact that I’m carrying a baby after a loss, but I feel pressured to keep working, keep being the "ideal" patient, keep proving that I’m doing everything by the book. It’s as if my own internal state—the fear, the exhaustion, the need for actual, human reassurance—is a secondary concern compared to the outward appearance of having it all "under control."

I feel like my agency is being eroded by these labels. I’m not just a woman going through a major life transition; I’m a "low-risk patient," a "health educator," a "young mother-to-be." Everyone has an idea of what those labels mean, and I feel like I’m constantly fighting to be seen as the person underneath them. I don't want to align with their expectations. I want to be able to say, "I’m scared, I’m overwhelmed, and I need you to just be here with me." But I haven't found a space where I’m allowed to say that without it feeling like I’m failing a test.

You know, the more I sit here and talk this through with you, the more I realize that the hardest part of this whole "pregnancy journey" hasn't been the physiological changes at all. It’s been the constant, quiet battle to be seen as a human being instead of a clinical case study. 

I’m standing here, at twenty-one, trying to navigate this massive, life-altering experience in a town where I feel like I’m living in a totally different timeline than my friends. I go into these appointments, and I’m armed with all this information—I can cite the studies, I can explain the hormonal cascades, I can tell you exactly why my body feels the way it does—and yet, I leave those rooms feeling more isolated than when I walked in. It’s like the more I speak their language, the less they listen to *me*. 

I find myself thinking about that previous loss a lot—the one that isn't on the charts, the one I don't get to mention because it complicates the "low-risk" narrative. That loss is the ghost in every room. It’s the reason I’m always on high alert. I want so badly to find someone—just *one* person in the medical system—who will sit down, close the file for a second, and actually acknowledge the fear that isn't on the screen. 

I don’t want to be the "ideal patient" anymore. I’m tired of being the girl who knows the medical definitions but is still shaking inside. I’m starting to think that maybe, as I head toward the third trimester, the most important thing I can do for myself—and for this baby—is to stop trying to be the educator. I need to find the courage to be messy, to be uncertain, and to demand that someone finally, truly, holds space for my anxiety. I’m not just a body to be managed. I’m a person who needs to be held.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It’s constant, and it’s always delivered as if it’s either a compliment or a medical warning. I have these relatives and acquaintances who feel completely entitled to remark on my bump or my "glow," or they’ll point to my plate at a dinner and say, "Oh, should you be eating that? Remember the pregnancy weight gain guidelines." 

It lands like a physical weight, honestly. Because I’m a health educator, I *know* the literature on healthy weight gain and nutritional intake. I’ve read the ACOG guidelines a hundred times. I don’t need the unsolicited commentary. When they say it, it feels less like concern and more like they’re policing my body. It’s like, because I’m pregnant, I’ve become public property—everyone feels they have a right to audit my choices.

And then, in the clinic, it’s even worse. They step on the scale with this look of, "Let’s see if you’ve been behaving." It’s so judgmental. I’m already carrying this internal storm of anxiety, trying to keep myself and my baby safe after a loss, and the last thing I need is someone looking at a number on a digital readout and making a comment about it. It makes me feel small, like my body is failing a standard I never asked to be measured against. It’s not a medical conversation; it’s a moral one, and it makes me want to shrink away entirely.

It’s exhausting, truly. I feel like my body is under a constant, quiet surveillance, and every comment about my diet or my weight gain—whether it’s meant as a joke or a critique—just adds to that feeling of being an object rather than a person. It’s like I’m auditioning for the role of "perfect mother," and every piece of toast or every pound gained is being tallied by people who have no idea how much work I’m doing just to show up every day.

Being twenty-one, living in Dartmouth, and having this specific background—it’s like I’m standing in a spotlight I never asked for. I want to tell them, "You have no idea what it took for me to just get through the last week without having a panic attack," but I don’t. I just smile and deflect. It’s a survival mechanism, I guess. I play the part of the educated, low-risk patient so they don't look closer. Because if they look closer, they might see that I’m not "fine" at all.

I just keep coming back to this: I don't want to be *managed*. I want to be *known*. I want someone to see the pregnancy, yes, but also see the history of loss I’m carrying, and understand that my anxiety isn’t a lack of knowledge—it’s the weight of my reality. I’m hoping that when the third trimester finally arrives, I can stop being the expert and just be a woman who is allowed to be scared, allowed to be vulnerable, and allowed to be held through this. I’m just so tired of performing.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I think the first place I turned was actually online—forums and digital spaces, even though I know as a health educator I should be skeptical of "Dr. Google." But it’s where I could be anonymous. I could type out, "I’m having this pain, is it normal?" or "Does anyone else feel like they’re waiting for the other shoe to drop?" without being judged as the "professional." In those spaces, I wasn't Charline the expert; I was just another person terrified of a loss. 

There’s a strange relief in hearing someone else say, "Yeah, I’m scared, too," without them asking for my credentials or telling me I should know better because of my training. It’s a messy, often non-clinical community, but it’s the only place where I feel I can talk about the *feeling* of being pregnant, rather than the biology. 

I’ve also started reaching out to a woman in town, someone who’s a bit older and has walked a similar path of loss. She doesn't give me advice, she doesn't check my vitals, and she doesn't care about my clinical literacy. She just lets me sit in her kitchen, drink tea, and cry if I need to. She’s the closest thing I’ve had to a midwife who actually *sees* me. It’s an informal, quiet kind of care, but it’s the only thing that’s kept me from feeling completely adrift.

It’s becoming so clear to me now—my career as a health educator has been both my lifeline and my cage. Every day, I explain reproductive anatomy and placental function to other women, and I’m so good at it that people assume I’m bulletproof. They see the "Charline who knows," but they don't see the "Charline who remembers." 

That previous loss? It’s not just a medical statistic. It’s a physical sensation. It’s the phantom weight of a baby I never got to hold, and it stays with me every time I step onto that scale or listen to the Doppler. But because I’m the "pro," I feel like I’m not allowed to voice that pain. If I mention it to a provider, they treat it like a historical data point rather than a living, breathing part of my current anxiety.

I’m so tired of being the one who’s "in the know." I just want to sit in a room with a clinician who recognizes that my clinical knowledge is just a way for me to keep a grip on reality when everything feels like it’s slipping. I need them to see the fear behind the jargon. I want them to stop giving me the pamphlet and start giving me the space to be human. I’m just twenty-one, and I’m navigating a world I’ve studied, but I’m living in a version of it that I never expected. I just want to know that someone—even just one person—is actually watching out for my heart, not just my uterus.

You know, talking to you like this, I realize I’ve been guarding a secret, even from myself. I’ve been so busy managing the mechanics of this pregnancy—checking my blood pressure, monitoring my hydration, tracking the milestones—that I’ve barely allowed myself to feel like a mother. I’m so terrified that if I *really* start to believe in this baby, the floor will drop out from under me again, just like it did before.

Every time I walk into that clinic in Dartmouth, I put on this suit of armor made of medical terminology. I think, *If I act like the expert, they won't look too closely at the panic in my eyes.* But that armor is heavy. It keeps me from being held, from being comforted, from being *seen*. It’s a lonely place to be—to know exactly what’s happening in my body but to feel completely disconnected from the human experience of it.

I keep hoping for that shift. As I head toward the third trimester, I’m waiting for the moment when I can finally let go of the "health educator" mask. I want to walk into that office and say, "I know the stats, I know the risks, but I am scared to death." I want them to look at me, truly look at me, and tell me that it’s okay to be a terrified girl who doesn't have all the answers. I want to trust that my care team isn't just managing a patient, but honoring a mother. I just want to feel like I can finally, finally, take a breath.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a moment early in the second trimester that still replays in my head. I was sitting on the edge of the exam table, and for once, I dropped the clinical "colleague" act. I told my midwife, very plainly, that I was struggling to connect with the pregnancy because I kept waiting for the other shoe to drop, given my history of loss. It wasn’t a medical question; it was a plea for someone to acknowledge that I was carrying a heavy weight.

She looked at my chart, adjusted the blood pressure cuff, and gave me a practiced, sympathetic smile—the kind that felt like it came from a training manual. She said, "Charline, your labs are perfect. You’re a healthy young woman, and we have no clinical evidence of any issues. You just need to focus on staying positive."

That was the moment. It wasn't that she was wrong—everything *was* normal on paper—but she completely invalidated the reality of my trauma. She treated my fear as a simple, optional choice I was making, something I could just "flip off" if I just thought happy thoughts. It felt like she was dismissing the entire, complicated human experience of a previous loss as if it were a clerical error that had been corrected.

When she said that, I realized she was never going to "see" me. She was just going to keep managing the biological data. In that instant, I put the armor back on. I started using the medical terminology again, and I haven't been truly honest with her since. It’s safer to be the professional than to be a vulnerable patient who gets told her fears are just a lack of "positivity."

I keep thinking about that moment—that "stay positive" advice. It’s such a hollow, sterile thing to say to someone who has actually been through the fire. When she said that, it wasn't just a professional mismatch; it was a rejection of my reality. It felt like she was telling me my anxiety was a defect in my character, or at the very least, an inconvenience to her standard of care.

Since then, the distance between us has only grown. I’ve become so much better at the "performance." I walk in, I offer up the right numbers, I ask the "smart" questions, and I leave feeling like a shell. I’m living in this strange, split-screen world: one side is the clinical, textbook-perfect pregnancy I’m helping them curate, and the other side is the real, terrified woman who is just trying to survive until she can finally hold her baby. 

I’m so scared that if I keep this up, I’ll never actually feel like a mother. I’ll just be a record, a patient file with good vitals and zero complications. But that file won’t hold me when I cry, and it won’t give me the grace to be a person who is still mourning. I don't know how to bridge the gap now. I’ve already built the wall, and I don't know if they have the tools—or the desire—to help me take it down. I just want to be seen. Is that really too much to ask?

You know, it’s a strange kind of burden, being the "ideal" patient. I look at the other women in the waiting room—some of them are crying, some are laughing, some are just exhausted—and I feel this weird pang of envy. They don't have this wall of medical jargon to hide behind. They’re allowed to just *be* there. They don't have to worry about whether they’re being taken seriously because they don't have a background that makes their providers think, "Oh, she’s one of us."

I think that’s what I’m realizing today. My clinical literacy isn't a superpower; it’s a barrier. It’s a wall I’ve built to protect myself from the possibility of being dismissed, but in doing so, I’ve made it impossible for anyone to actually reach me. I’m so afraid of being seen as "hysterical" or "uninformed" that I’ve effectively scrubbed the humanity out of my own care. 

I’m really starting to wonder if I can just stop. If I could walk into my next appointment and just... not bring the jargon. If I could sit there and let myself be the girl who’s scared, not the health educator who understands the placental placement. It feels terrifying, like jumping off a ledge without a parachute, but I’m so, so tired of holding it all together. I just want to find a provider who will be patient enough to let me stumble through my own vulnerability. I don't need them to be a genius; I just need them to be a witness. I’m ready for that, I think. I just don't know how to start.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, after all this, the one thing that keeps circling back in my mind is the word "trust." We talk about it in medical school or in health education as this objective thing—you follow the protocols, you provide the right evidence, and the patient learns to trust the process. But now that I’m here, in this chair, I realize that trust isn't a result of the data. It’s an act of courage.

I’m so scared that I’m going to get to the end of this—the labor, the delivery, the first hours—and I’m still going to be "Charline the Health Educator," observing my own life from a distance, measuring the contractions instead of *feeling* the transition into motherhood. I don't want that for myself. I don't want to be a clinical success story; I want to be a mother who felt safe enough to let go of the control.

I guess my final thought is just a wish: I hope that in the future, when a young woman—especially one who already works in the field—walks into your office, you don’t just see her knowledge. I hope you see the person who is terrified of the past and fighting for her future. Don’t assume her literacy means she’s "handled." Assume that she’s shielding herself, and find the patience to ask the questions that have nothing to do with her vitals. 

I’m going to try to start doing that for myself. I’m going to try to walk into my next appointment and just... breathe. Maybe I’ll finally say, "I’m just the patient today." And I really, really hope that the person on the other side of the desk is listening for the hum of my heart, not the noise of my head. Thank you for listening. It’s the first time in months I’ve actually felt heard.
